questions.question: Why can a small cache hit-rate improvement matter a lot in edge systems?
options: Because even a 1% to 2% shift can remove a very large absolute number of origin requests and miss penalties at high traffic scale.
Because hit rate only affects dashboard appearance, not system behavior.
Because higher hit rate always means lower purge complexity.
Because origin costs disappear completely after any improvement.
questions.explanation: At massive traffic volumes, small percentages correspond to meaningful infrastructure and latency changes.

questions.question: What is the main reason cost discussions belong in an edge design interview answer?
options: Because edge footprint, routing, and cache policy choices can materially change egress, origin load, and operational complexity.
Because cost only matters after the system is fully launched.
Because interviewers care only about cheaper systems, not reliable ones.
Because latency and cost are never connected.
questions.explanation: Strong designs acknowledge the real business and operational cost of their choices.

questions.question: Why should incident-time traffic shifts be part of cost modeling?
options: Failover paths can create large temporary egress and origin-capacity spikes that normal-state averages hide.
Incidents do not affect network cost significantly.
Because cost modeling should ignore degraded mode to stay simple.
Because latency is irrelevant during failover.
questions.explanation: The most expensive path may appear only during incidents, which is exactly when it matters.

questions.question: What hidden cost often appears when expanding cacheability aggressively?
options: Purge and invalidation complexity can rise sharply even while hit rate improves.
Origin load always increases instead of decreases.
Regional shields stop providing value.
Routing policy becomes simpler automatically.
questions.explanation: Caching more data is not free if keeping it fresh becomes much harder.

questions.question: Which metric best validates whether a proposed edge optimization actually paid off?
options: Hit rate and origin QPS avoided by route class, paired with latency deltas.
Only the total number of POPs in the network.
The number of internal documents written about the feature.
TLS certificate chain length.
questions.explanation: You need both performance and avoided-origin-load evidence, ideally by traffic class.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of prioritizing premium low-latency routing for a subset of traffic?
options: It can improve important user paths, but increases routing complexity and possibly network spend for the fleet.
It lowers cost and complexity for every other tenant automatically.
It removes the need for cache hit-rate analysis.
It guarantees the best incident behavior in all regions.
questions.explanation: Differentiated routing can be valuable, but it is not operationally free.

questions.question: If origin capacity is cheap but user latency is extremely valuable, what is the strongest design instinct?
options: Optimize the user-visible hot path deliberately while still making purge and control-plane cost visible.
Ignore cost modeling entirely because latency is valuable.
Disable all cache layers to simplify the answer.
Assume every route should receive identical treatment.
questions.explanation: Valuable latency improvements can justify spend, but the answer should still quantify the trade.

questions.question: Why is a global average cache hit rate often misleading in design review?
options: Different route classes and object shapes can have very different economics and freshness constraints, so averages hide the real trade-offs.
Because global averages are mathematically invalid.
Because origin load depends only on median latency.
Because purge cost is unrelated to traffic shape.
questions.explanation: Useful cost and latency analysis should usually be segmented by traffic class.
